skill.name: ai-content-pipeline
skill.description: Build repeatable AI content workflows that combine text generation, image generation, video animation, voiceover, and media merging through the inference.sh CLI.
skill.summary: Create multi-step AI media pipelines for scripts, images, video, voice, and final edits.
skill.author: inference-sh

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly identified one high-risk install pattern: line 21 pipes a remote URL directly into sh. Most Ruby backtick and weak cryptography findings are false positives caused by Markdown fences, inline model names, descriptions, and links. No evidence found of malicious intent, credential exfiltration, or prompt injection, but publication should wait until the unsafe installer guidance is replaced.

high_findings.title: Remote Script Piped Directly To Shell
high_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: The quick start command downloads a remote installer and pipes it directly into sh. This executes unverified network content with the user's local permissions before review.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact command uses curl from https://cli.inference.sh and pipes the result to sh. This is a well-known unsafe installation pattern even when the vendor is legitimate.

medium_findings.title: Workflows Send User Content To External AI Services
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: The examples run remote inference applications for script writing, image generation, text-to-speech, avatar video, upscaling, and media merging. This is the core purpose of the skill, but users may send private scripts, source text, image URLs, or audio URLs to third-party services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill repeatedly invokes infsh app run with provider apps and user-supplied prompts or media URLs. The behavior is explicit and legitimate, but it creates privacy and data handling risk.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The static analyzer flagged Markdown code fences and inline model identifiers as Ruby or shell backtick execution. SKILL.md is documentation, and these locations do not execute Ruby backticks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are Markdown fences, table cells, or inline command names, not executable Ruby code. Actual shell execution risk is separately captured at line 21.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Mostly Benign References
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE except for the installer URL on line 21: the other hardcoded URLs are the vendor site, documentation links, and a placeholder music URL. No evidence found of credential exfiltration endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation or placeholder references and are not combined with secrets. The only unsafe network URL is already reported as the shell installer issue.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The flagged locations are descriptive text and a workflow documentation URL. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, insecure cipher use, or cryptographic code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines shows front matter description and a documentation link. There is no cryptographic implementation at either location.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe To Shell Installation Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The command combines network download and shell execution in a single step. Users should download, inspect, pin, or use a package-managed installation path instead.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is directly present as curl piped into sh. It can execute arbitrary installer content if the remote endpoint or connection is compromised.

content.value_statement: Creators need repeatable ways to turn ideas into finished media across several AI tools. This skill organizes script, image, video, audio, and merge steps into clear inference.sh workflows.
actual_capabilities: Plans chained workflows for script generation, voiceover, image generation, animation, and media merging.
Provides examples for YouTube Shorts, talking head videos, product demos, and blog-to-video conversion.
Maps content tasks to specific inference.sh apps such as FLUX, Wan, Kokoro TTS, OmniHuman, and media merger.
Shows how to save intermediate outputs so each pipeline stage can be reviewed or reused.
Documents related skills for video generation, image generation, text-to-speech, LLM models, and the inference.sh platform.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI, authentication, and access to the referenced remote apps.
Does not automatically verify licensing, consent, or usage rights for generated voices, images, music, or source material.
Outputs depend on external model availability, provider policies, queue times, and account limits.
Captioning, quality review, and final editorial decisions still require human oversight.

use_cases.title: Produce Short Social Videos
use_cases.description: Turn a topic into a script, narration, animated background, and merged short-form video.

use_cases.title: Create Product Demo Assets
use_cases.description: Generate product visuals, animate a reveal, upscale the result, and combine it with music.

use_cases.title: Repurpose Articles Into Video
use_cases.description: Summarize written content into key points, create visuals for each point, and assemble a narrated video.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Simple Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan a simple AI content pipeline for a 30-second video about [topic]. Include the script, voice, image, animation, and merge steps.

prompt_templates.title: Adapt a Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Adapt the YouTube Short pipeline for [platform] with a [tone] style, [aspect ratio], and [target audience]. Explain each step before running commands.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Batch Content Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a repeatable pipeline that converts [number] source ideas into short videos. Include naming, intermediate review points, and quality checks.

prompt_templates.title: Audit and Improve a Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my current AI media pipeline for privacy, cost, quality, and failure points. Suggest a safer and more reliable sequence using inference.sh apps.

output_examples.input: A creator asks for a 30-second educational video about renewable energy.
output: A concise script step for the narration.
A voiceover generation step with a selected voice.
A visual prompt for the background image.
An animation step for subtle camera motion.
A final merge step that combines video and narration.

output_examples.input: A marketer wants a product reveal for wireless earbuds.
output: A video upscaling step for final quality.
A music merge step with reduced audio volume.

output_examples.input: A team wants to turn a blog post into a narrated explainer.
output: A summary stage with five key points.
A visual generation stage for each point.
An image animation stage for each scene.
A narration and final assembly stage.

best_practices: Review source material for privacy, consent, and rights before sending it to remote AI services.
Save and inspect intermediate outputs before using them in later pipeline stages.
Start with fast draft settings, then use higher quality models for approved final assets.
anti_patterns: Do not send confidential scripts, unreleased product details, or private media without approval.
Do not run remote installer commands without inspecting and pinning the installation source.
Do not merge generated assets into final content without checking accuracy, licensing, and brand suitability.

faq.question: What does this skill help me create?
faq.answer: It helps plan and run AI workflows for videos, voiceovers, product demos, social posts, and repurposed written content.

faq.question: Does it generate media by itself?
faq.answer: No. It uses the inference.sh CLI to call external apps that generate scripts, images, video, audio, and merged media.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What setup is required?
faq.answer: You need the inference.sh CLI, an authenticated account, and access to the specific apps used in the workflow.

faq.question: Is it safe for private content?
faq.answer: Use caution. The workflows can send prompts, text, image URLs, and audio URLs to remote AI services.

faq.question: Can it handle final publishing?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on content generation and assembly. Scheduling, distribution, and policy review are outside its scope.
